# rishi


<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

This file will become your README and also the index of your documentation.

## Developer Guide

If you are new to using `nbdev` here are some useful pointers to get you started.

### Install rishi in Development mode

``` sh
# make sure rishi package is installed in development mode
$ pip install -e .

# make changes under nbs/ directory
# ...

# compile to have changes apply to rishi
$ nbdev_prepare
```

## Usage

### Installation

Install latest from the GitHub [repository](https://github.com/vedicreader/rishi):

``` sh
$ pip install git+https://github.com/vedicreader/rishi.git
```

or from [conda](https://anaconda.org/vedicreader/rishi)

``` sh
$ conda install -c vedicreader rishi
```

or from [pypi](https://pypi.org/project/rishi/)

``` sh
$ pip install rishi
```

### Documentation

Documentation can be found hosted on this GitHub [repository](https://github.com/vedicreader/rishi)’s [pages](https://vedicreader.github.io/rishi/). Additionally you can find package manager specific guidelines on [conda](https://anaconda.org/vedicreader/rishi) and [pypi](https://pypi.org/project/rishi/) respectively.

## How to use

[`Chat`](https://vedicreader.github.io/rishi/core.html#chat) wraps a local litert_lm Gemma engine with a callable turn interface, message history, an ordered callback system, streaming, and token-usage tracking (so you know when to compress the conversation):

``` python
from rishi.core import Chat

chat = Chat()                       # downloads gemma-4-E2B on first use
print(chat("Hi, who are you?"))     # one turn -> litert response dict

for md in chat("Now stream a haiku", stream=True): print(md, end='')  # streaming

chat.use               # this turn's UsageStats: total/in/out tokens
chat.token_count, chat.pct_full     # live context size vs ctx_limit -> when to compress
```
